frameworks.name: holistic Bayesian matching
frameworks.one_line: multi-level objectives across a representational hierarchy explain choice-induced estimation biases

frameworks.name: self-consistent / confirmation bias
frameworks.one_line: prior categorical choice biases subsequent perceptual estimate

frameworks.name: category-level stimulus representation
frameworks.one_line: reference induces implicit category representation even without an explicit choice

core_question: Do retrospective versus prospective categorical choices induce different biases on subsequent perceptual estimates of the same stimulus?
key_assumptions: categorical choices can bias subsequent continuous estimates
identical stimulus replay isolates choice-induced bias from sampling noise
the reference itself can induce a category-level representation
manipulations: timing of the categorical decision: after 4 samples (prospective) vs after 8 samples (retrospective)
generative mean angular position relative to reference
key_findings: robust estimation biases away from the reference in both conditions
nearly identical estimate distributions across prospective and retrospective conditions
correlation between categorical choice and subsequent estimate was strongly reduced in the prospective condition
interpretations: the mere presence of a reference induces an implicit category-level representation that biases estimates even without an explicit prior choice
a holistic Bayesian model with multi-level objectives across the representational hierarchy accounts for the observed pattern
